quotes.verbatim_quote: Traditional configurations are well known; nontraditional configurations must be found, explored, analyzed, and well practiced.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the chapter's rationale for drilling non-idiomatic triad/bass alignments: traditional voicings need no extra work, so practice time must target the unusual.

quotes.verbatim_quote: It is not good to shy away from dissonances. Let tones clash at times:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the dissonance-tolerance rule that licenses Van Eps's chromatic contrary-motion exercises, where clashes are pedagogical features not bugs.

quotes.topic: Mechanical motion regardless of harmonic result
quotes.verbatim_quote:
This type of chordal movement is based on mathematical
mechanical motion without regard for the harmonic result,
which produces many interesting relationships, and provides
very useful fingerboard knowledge. Notice the very interest-
ing dissonances and progressions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the chapter's generative method: drive triads and bass by independent mechanical rules and harvest the harmonic byproducts.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Extend all of the examples to cover all of the 12 keys.

Make up other patterns, select other keys, change the order of
the triads, change the bass line also. Then, for the sake of
discipline, don’t deviate from that format. Stick to it, no
matter how dissonant it sounds

Later on, when the principle is understood more clearly,
deviation should take place.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the two-stage learning rule—strict adherence first, deviation only after mastery—that governs how the student practices every chromatic-triad pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The bass lines are completely independent of the triads. The
bass line is an independent fourth voice, therefore it will use
its own accidentals and because of this, there will be a
mixture of accidentals. This situation occurs when working
with more than one key simultaneously because, one can't
use multiple signatures.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the load-bearing premise that the bass and triad are notationally independent voices in two keys at once, explaining the chapter's mixed-accidental enharmonics.

quotes.topic: Triad and bass as separate identities
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Always think of the triad and bass as being separate
isolated lines in an overall four note harmonic group of
tones. It is very important that they keep their identities at all
time while still becoming one overall sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Encodes the analytical-listening doctrine the student must hold while practicing: two simultaneous monophonic lines that incidentally form four-note chords.

quotes.topic: Major makes minor, minor makes major
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The minor seventh chord has to contain the notes (open or closed) of one of the three pure major triads.
The major seventh chord has to contain the notes of one of the pure minor triads—in other words, major makes minor, and minor
makes major.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the harmonic identity rule the chapter exploits: seventh chords decompose into a triad-of-the-opposite-modality, which justifies the entire Major/Minor Series cross-mapping.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The letter *

the end of a chord symbol designates the bass note in the chord. Example:

If the bass is the third of the chord the last two figures would be the number three and the letter “B” =

3B etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the '3B' bass-position symbol used throughout the Major/Minor Series, without which the chord labels in the chapter are unreadable.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The inversion arpeggios help in finding the recognizable overall chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the analytical use of inversion arpeggios: rearranging triad voices against a bass exposes which familiar 4-note chord is actually present.

quotes.topic: Cycle of fifths bass is neutral
quotes.verbatim_quote: A bass line descending through the cycle of fifths is neutral—neither major or minor—until other voices are introduced.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies why the circle-5 bass is Van Eps's preferred generator: its modal neutrality allows it to host both major and minor triad streams without bias.

quotes.verbatim_quote: All small chords have second, third, fourth (etc) callings under varied harmonic conditions, positions and situations.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Encodes the multi-naming doctrine—the same notes belong to many chord names depending on context—that the chapter trains via its dual-key triad/bass labels.

quotes.topic: Dissonance is natural in contrary motion
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Dissonances are natural when working with chromatic contrary motion involving a chord line
going one way and the bass line going another.

Also, in moving a chord chromatically up or down against a pedal bass note.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the inevitable dissonance of contrary-motion and pedal-bass material so the student doesn't 'correct' the exercises out of existence.
